Author: Anindya Chakraborty
Title: A Beginner’s Guide to AI Assisted Coding
Text:
A Beginner’s Guide to AI Assisted Coding — Part I
A comprehensive guide for software engineers venturing into using AI coding agents — Part I
You can find the Part II here
There is a lot going on with AI Coding assistants. There is too much hype involving many products and technologies with a new buzzy name coming in the market every other day. It can be hard to keep track, especially if you have not been paying attention. As an engineer all you care about is getting your job done with good quality, doesn’t matter what tool gets you there. This guide will try to give a comprehensive overview of all the buzzy terms and different products, which one to use where and point to some of the best practices and pitfalls people have identified. Part I covers the different kinds of tools and their use cases. Part II will go in details of better context + MCPs, best practices and the challenges and strengths these tools have demonstrated.
The Different AI Coding Tools
While from a feature perspective all the major vendors are starting to converge, there are few distinct types of interactions they provide. I think a pattern is emerging of which one is the most useful when, but I will leave it to you to explore and find out what works for you.
Smart Autocomplete — This is everything from finishing the function signature to completing a mapper method to map one type to another. This was the first common AI coding usage mostly popularized by Github Copilot and almost all IDEs now support this in some form or fashion. This continues to be the most widely used and trusted usages of AI assisted coding.
Smart IDEs — Cursor, Windsurf etc. are the most popular in this category, while VSCode with Copilot is getting to that point. Even Claude Code provides IDE integrations to make legacy IDEs smarter. Beyond autocomplete they usually provide a chat interface in one shape or another for you to ask questions about code, explore options and generate code either in a given context (hence the name “Cursor”?) or more broadly.
Command Line Agents — This is a new-old thing. Aider (and some other open source coding agents) were already doing this for last couple of years, but never caught on to wider attention. Claude Code made this cool (and yes Claude Code can be pretty fun to use at times). They always operate over the complete repo (or provided context) and identify related context from your prompts. They can also be very useful if you are trying to pipe information to them and process large scale streamed information like logs or really large files.
Background Agents — This one is a bit more of a recent development. These are background agent tasks that you fire off for passive, potentially long running tasks like identifying and refactoring tech debt, version update of some specific libraries, fixing a backlog of bugs etc.
Fullstack Managed App Generation — Companies like Replit, V0 (and a bunch of others) provide low or no-code options where you vibe code an entire app that’s hosted by them. So you don’t need to know or understand the infra, databases and whatnot. Usually these are good options for generating prototype SaaS products and internal/low volume tools that historically has been hard to fund in bigger companies. I don’t see a lot of software engineers using these tools and most of the time their target audience are different.
Use Cases
Before we go into how to utilize these tools the best, it’s important to talk about where these agents are seeing the most usage and trust.
Documentation, Code Explanation — Being driven by LLMs mean these AI coding tools inherently are good at language translation tasks. So it’s not surprising that almost anyone interacting with a codebase can find use of the AI coding tools in understanding a codebase, map out a complex flow, extract an architecture diagram and such. Most of these tools are used both for generating docs and to chat about the codebase.
End-to-end Application Generation — You need to build a prototype or a decent baseline for a library? Several AI coding agents have proven themselves useful in this space. Devin pioneered this use case, but faced some serious challenges afterwards. Platforms like Replit took this one step further and provided app generation for a standard stack. But many software engineers are using Claude Code or Cursor (and some more) to quickly generate the first version of an app or service or library with all the boilerplate and basic functionality so that they can build from there. In most cases an engineer doesn’t know, care or remember each and every detail of setting up a framework. These agents make that extremely easy by generating all the boilerplate.
In most cases an engineer doesn’t know, care or remember each and every detail of setting up a framework. These agents make that extremely easy by generating all the boilerplate.
Researching and Building Integrations — Want to integrate Postmark for an email sending use case? Traditionally that would mean investigating all the endpoints, figuring out the one you need, writing all the code to build their payload etc. and integrating them in the existing code base. Now all you need is a simple prompt, the agents can research the documentation, extract the relevant endpoints, identify the integration points, generate the mappers and get you a pretty good first version ready to be tested.
Analyzing Logs — Pipe your service logs to Claude Code, give them access to the code, prompt for what you are looking for and let the agent handle the rest. This is a pretty common use I am seeing again and again. But keep in mind, AI coding agents reduce your work doesn’t mean they are a silver bullet. All the good practices about generating logs apply irrespective of you will read them vs. agents.
This is a new space and I am sure I missed several common usages. And I am also sure there are a lot more use cases yet to come.
Before you go (and hopefully come back for the Part II), a word of caution. AI Coding Agents are not magic. They are bound by all the rules of software engineering (and Physics). On top, while they are doing some phenomenal stuff pretty consistently, there is no alternative (yet) for human supervision and ingenuity. So don’t blindly trust their output, either the code or the insights they generate.
